Ideological congressional caucuses represent factions within a political party. These congressional caucuses help congregate and advance the ideals of more focused ideologies within the two major [big-tent](/wiki/Big-tent "Big-tent") political parties. Some caucuses are organized [political factions](/wiki/Political_faction "Political faction") with a common ideological orientation.[[126]](#cite_note-Congressional_caucus_Hawkings_2016-131) Most ideological caucuses are confined to the House of Representatives. The rosters of large caucuses are usually listed publicly. Members of Congress are not restricted to a single ideological caucus, creating overlaps between the organizations. Some ideological caucuses exercise influence akin to that of [whips](/wiki/Whip_(politics) "Whip (politics)").

## Legislative functions

Most bills may be introduced in either House of Congress. However, the Constitution states, "All Bills for raising Revenue shall originate in the House of Representatives." Because of the [Origination Clause](/wiki/Origination_Clause "Origination Clause"), the Senate cannot initiate bills imposing taxes. This provision barring the Senate from introducing revenue bills is based on the practice of the [British Parliament](/wiki/British_Parliament "British Parliament"), in which only the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") may originate such measures. Congressional tradition holds that the House of Representatives originates [appropriation bills](/wiki/Appropriation_bill "Appropriation bill").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Although it cannot originate revenue bills, the Senate retains the power to amend or reject them. [Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson") wrote the following about appropriations bills:[[127]](#cite_note-132)

> [T]he constitutional prerogative of the House has been held to apply to all the general appropriations bills, and the Senate's right to amend these has been allowed the widest possible scope. The upper house may add to them what it pleases; may go altogether outside of their original provisions and tack to them entirely new features of legislation, altering not only the amounts but even the objects of expenditure, and making out of the materials sent them by the popular chamber measures of an almost totally new character.

The approval of the Senate and the House of Representatives is required for a bill to become law. Both Houses must pass the same version of the bill. If there are differences, they may be resolved by a [conference committee](/wiki/Conference_committee "Conference committee"), which includes members of both bodies.[[128]](#cite_note-133)

The president may [veto](/wiki/Veto_power_in_the_United_States "Veto power in the United States") a bill passed by the House and Senate. If they do, the bill does not become law unless each House, by a two-thirds vote, votes to override the veto. The House and Senate cannot override a [pocket veto](/wiki/Pocket_veto "Pocket veto").[[129]](#cite_note-134)

## Checks and balances

The Constitution provides that the Senate's "[advice and consent](/wiki/Advice_and_consent "Advice and consent")" is necessary for the president to make appointments and to ratify treaties.[[130]](#cite_note-135)

The Constitution empowers the House of Representatives to [impeach](/wiki/Impeach "Impeach") federal officials for "[Treason](/wiki/Treason "Treason"), [Bribery](/wiki/Bribery "Bribery"), or other high [Crimes](/wiki/Crime "Crime") and [Misdemeanors](/wiki/Misdemeanor "Misdemeanor")" and empowers the Senate to try such impeachments.[[131]](#cite_note-136) The House may approve "articles of impeachment" by a simple majority vote. A two-thirds vote is required for conviction in the Senate.[[132]](#cite_note-137) A convicted official is automatically removed from office and may be disqualified from holding future office under the United States.[[133]](#cite_note-138) No further punishment is permitted during the impeachment proceedings; however, the party may face criminal penalties in a normal court of law.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In U.S. history, the House of Representatives [has impeached seventeen officials](/wiki/Federal_impeachment_in_the_United_States#List_of_federal_impeachments "Federal impeachment in the United States"), seven of whom were convicted. Another, [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon"), resigned after the [House Judiciary Committee](/wiki/House_Judiciary_Committee "House Judiciary Committee") passed [articles of impeachment](/wiki/Articles_of_impeachment "Articles of impeachment") but before a formal impeachment vote by the full House. Only three U.S. presidents have ever been impeached: [Andrew Johnson](/wiki/Andrew_Johnson "Andrew Johnson") in 1868, [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton") in 1998, and [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") in 2019 and in 2021.[[134]](#cite_note-139) The trials of Johnson, Clinton, and Trump all ended in acquittal.[[135]](#cite_note-140) In Johnson's case, the Senate fell one vote short of the two-thirds majority required for conviction.[[136]](#cite_note-141)

Under the [Twelfth Amendment](/wiki/Twelfth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Twelfth Amendment to the United States Constitution"), the House has the power to elect the president if no presidential candidate receives a majority of votes in the [Electoral College](/wiki/Electoral_College_(United_States) "Electoral College (United States)"). The Twelfth Amendment requires the House to choose from the three candidates with the highest numbers of electoral votes. The Constitution provides that "the votes shall be taken by states, the representation from each state having one vote".[[137]](#cite_note-142)

It is rare for no presidential candidate to receive a majority of electoral votes. In U.S. history, the House has only had to choose a president twice. In the [1800 election](/wiki/1800_United_States_presidential_election "1800 United States presidential election"), which was held before adoption of the [Twelfth Amendment](/wiki/Twelfth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Twelfth Amendment to the United States Constitution"), it elected [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson") over [Aaron Burr](/wiki/Aaron_Burr "Aaron Burr"). In the [1824 election](/wiki/1824_United_States_presidential_election "1824 United States presidential election"), it elected [John Quincy Adams](/wiki/John_Quincy_Adams "John Quincy Adams") over [Andrew Jackson](/wiki/Andrew_Jackson "Andrew Jackson") and [William H. Crawford](/wiki/William_H._Crawford "William H. Crawford"). If no vice presidential candidate receives a majority of the electoral votes, the Senate elects the vice president from the two candidates with the highest numbers of electoral votes.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Before the [Twenty-fifth Amendment](/wiki/Twenty-fifth_Amendment "Twenty-fifth Amendment") was adopted in 1967, if the office of the vice president became prematurely vacant, it was left vacant until the next inauguration.[[138]](#cite_note-143)
Since then, both House and Senate confirmation is required to fill a vacancy if the vice presidency is vacant.[[139]](#cite_note-144)

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-3)** This emblem appears on the flag and is sometimes referred to as the seal of the House. However, the actual seal depicts the central part of the [United States Capitol](/wiki/United_States_Capitol "United States Capitol")[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-4)** [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska") (for its primary elections only), [California](/wiki/California "California"), and [Washington](/wiki/Washington_(state) "Washington (state)") additionally utilize a [nonpartisan blanket primary](/wiki/Nonpartisan_blanket_primary "Nonpartisan blanket primary"), and [Mississippi](/wiki/Mississippi "Mississippi") uses the [two-round system](/wiki/Two-round_system "Two-round system"), for their respective [primary elections](/wiki/Primary_elections "Primary elections").
3. **[^](#cite_ref-16)** Not to be confused with [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation"), an electoral system that typically elects [multiple members](/wiki/Electoral_system#Multi-winner_systems "Electoral system") of a party to a legislature based on that party's percentage of a popular vote.
4. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-RSCRGG_129-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-RSCRGG_129-1) The Republican Study Committee no longer publishes a detailed member roster and the actual count may therefore be higher.
5. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-FC_130-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-FC_130-1) The Freedom caucus does not publish its member roster and the actual count may therefore be higher.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-145)** The number of the majority party's voting representatives in the House in excess of the minimum number required to have an absolute majority of voting representatives.

"While some believed the Articles should be 'corrected and enlarged as to accomplish the objects proposed by their institution,' the Virginia Plan called for completely replacing it with a strong central government based on popular consent and proportional representation.... The Virginia Plan received support from states with large populations such as Pennsylvania, Massachusetts, and South Carolina. A number of smaller states, however, proposed the 'New Jersey Plan,' drafted by William Paterson, which retained the essential features of the original Articles: a unicameral legislature where all states had equal representation, the appointment of a plural executive, and a supreme court of limited jurisdiction.... The committee's report, dubbed the Great Compromise, ironed out many contentious points. It resolved the delegates' sharpest disagreement by prescribing a bicameral legislature with proportional representation in the House and equal state representation in the Senate. After two more months of intense debates and revisions, the delegates produced the document we now know as the Constitution, which expanded the power of the central government while protecting the prerogatives of the states."
